title: Instrumentalities and running away
wikitext:
[]: Instrumentalities and running away             Quote Post by Kendrix » Sat May 17, 2014 5:51 pm Split from [url]http://forum.evageeks.org/thread/1263/Shinjis-Dark-Secret[/url] xanderkh wrote: Yet again, just another aspect of Shinji's hypocritical nature. All throughout the series he bouts not to run away; but yet again chooses to try to escape not only reality but the people around him, rather than facing the truth.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Sat May 17, 2014 7:45 pm Wow, Anno. I seriously can't disagree more with the idea that 'it is okay to run away' is a message the show puts forth. In just about every possible situation running away either is or would have been the worst possible choice. This sort of philosophy only works when you're talking about an individual who does NOT have a duty to others, much less the fate of the whole damn species hanging on their actions.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Sat May 17, 2014 8:34 pm NemZ wrote: Wow, Anno. I seriously can't disagree more with the idea that 'it is okay to run away' is a message the show puts forth. In just about every possible situation running away either is or would have been the worst possible choice.

[]:             Quote Post by Stryker » Sat May 17, 2014 8:57 pm As you said though, F-4 (for the Four F's in your name) NGE is in fact telling its viewers that it is indeed okay to run away from time to time, just so long as you know what else it is that you would be running to as a result.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Sat May 17, 2014 9:21 pm You know, for as often as we talking about Shinji running away, I guess we should also use the visuals Anno gives us as references. Anno uses the Exit Sign in both EoTV and EoE. In EoTV, the Exit Sign is in the auditorium, an auditorium that cracks around him as he learns to accept himself. In that sense, he runs away front he auditorium to self acceptance. In EoE, the Exit Sign flashes just before Shinji goes into his mental breakdown and hears the voices of the women and girls in his life berating him. This probably suggests that running into Instrumentality is to run away from real life. But later on, we see Shinji use phrases that are very dismissive and almost spiteful towards the idea of Instrumentality, and even the purpose behind Rei and Kaworu. This tone leads us to believe that he is running away yet again. (The visual of him drifting away from his mother especially invokes the feeling of running away, or at least leaving something.) It appears as though, in both in endings, Shinji realizes that no matter what he does, he will be running away from something. So, whereas it is a contradiction of his statements at the beginning of the series “I mustn’t run away,” it’s a contradiction that he realizes he’ll be making no matter what he chooses. Either that, or Shinji doesn’t realize this, but the show itself does. Both EoTV and parts of EoE have a very impressionistic style to them, where the film would show flashes of ones’ subconscious thoughts of the character, or even hidden meanings that the character doesn’t even realize are around him. It could be that the show itself is telling us that Shinji is running away, even though that, in EoTV at least, Shinji doesn’t seem to realize that. (In EoE Shinji definitely realizes that he’s running away.)

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Sat May 17, 2014 10:23 pm I would have to disagree with that interpretation. In 3 Misato is actually most pissed off not because he disobeyed her order (which he should NOT have done, since he was gambling the whole operation on a very risky attack and just got lucky) but because he doesn't seem to care. In other words MINDFUL disobedience is preferable to MINDLESS anything here, because even if it worked this time that sort of attitude is sure to undermine their efforts going forwards. In 18 he had freedom to act and exercised that freedom to refuse doing so. Again it's his inability to see the larger picture and adamant refusal to consider that his naive moral stance might be wrong (which honestly in this case is less about protecting anyone and more about keeping his own hands clean) is the real problem. Gendo actually facilitated his choice here, letting him have his personal safe zone while insuring things would proceed as they needed to regardless of his childish tantrums. Shinji's view that his father 'used his hands' here is incorrect, and it doesn't really matter if Yui disagrees on principle or not... the fact is Gendo saved the world (and Toji) while Shinji did nothing. It's arguable that his choice in 24 is his highest point in the series as it demonstrates that his choice to return and be a badass in 19 wasn't a fluke and he's finally learned his lesson and taken responsibility for his own actions as they affect the world around him even when that decision has obvious negative costs. 25 and 26 is the process of taking that lesson and learning that it can be applied to other aspects of his life as well, extending as far as changing reality itself in EoTV. Making a choice is the point, even if that choice is to withdraw from a situation. Refusing to act is the sort of running away that Shinji struggles with, hoping things will just work out if he stays out of the way, or at the very least that he won't be blamed as long as he doesn't get involved. That's why EoE Shinji is such a detestable little shit. He refuses to take any action, begs others to make choices for him when it's all too late to do anything anyway, then decides to throw it all away so that there are no more consequences at all... and when that doesn't work out to his liking he just wants to go back to how it was because then it will once again be the comfortable old status quo where he doesn't have to answer for letting everyone die and blowing up the world. Fuck that guy. FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: So the question after that is “Does NGE give us two endings where both are not the ‘good’ or ‘moral' ending?"

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Sun May 18, 2014 4:54 am Kendrix wrote: The red/blue scene, in particular, comes with this bit of text abou how he can't possibly understand her if she keeps pushing him away, intrerestingly abit of a reversal of a similar dialogue in EoTv - Not that this is that surpising, since both could do with making their intentions more clear, but let us not throw all nuance overboard and confuse with "You might have gotten closer to what you wanted with making your intentions more clear" with the "You could easily have solved all of the other persons problems and you didn't." that is being thrown around here.

[]:             Quote Post by ElMariachi » Sun May 18, 2014 5:20 am NemZ wrote: In 18 he had freedom to act and exercised that freedom to refuse doing so. Again it's his inability to see the larger picture and adamant refusal to consider that his naive moral stance might be wrong (which honestly in this case is less about protecting anyone and more about keeping his own hands clean) is the real problem. Gendo actually facilitated his choice here, letting him have his personal safe zone while insuring things would proceed as they needed to regardless of his childish tantrums. Shinji's view that his father 'used his hands' here is incorrect, and it doesn't really matter if Yui disagrees on principle or not... the fact is Gendo saved the world (and Toji) while Shinji did nothing.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Sun May 18, 2014 5:32 am ElMariachi wrote: I was under the impression that he judged Instrumentality (like Rei/Lilith kinda subtly incited him to do), found that it sucked and wasn't good for mankind and aborted the thing, and he was ready to face the consequences of his acts : yeah he blew up the world and tanged everyone, and will have to explain that to the people that will come back, Asuka's "Kimochi Warui" was here to show that people still remember what happened. If he really wanted to avoid the consequences of his actions, he would had stayed inside Instrumentality instead of reconstituting himself.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Sun May 18, 2014 5:36 am ElMariachi wrote: I disagree here

[]:             Quote Post by ElMariachi » Sun May 18, 2014 6:14 am NemZ wrote: 18, not 19. I was talking about his refusing to fight back against Bardiel, not running away afterwards.

[]:             Quote Post by KingXanaduu » Sun May 18, 2014 8:26 am Kendrix wrote: 'Hypocritical' is a really cold and unforgiving way to put it that completely misses the point of the character; There is hypocricy in this world, it's one of the lowest and filthiest things, but this is not what that word means. He's not going out and claiming that he is someone who never runs or laim to be better than others because of this, rather, it's the exact oposite. He's simply aware that he could do some things better, but doesn't always manage to live up to perfection.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Sun May 18, 2014 4:52 pm NemZ wrote: In 3 Misato is actually most pissed off not because he disobeyed her order (which he should NOT have done, since he was gambling the whole operation on a very risky attack and just got lucky) but because he doesn't seem to care. In other words MINDFUL disobedience is preferable to MINDLESS anything here, because even if it worked this time that sort of attitude is sure to undermine their efforts going forwards.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Sun May 18, 2014 11:28 pm FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: So, are you saying it would have been okay for Shinji to run away from the Angel at this point?

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Sun May 18, 2014 11:39 pm NemZ wrote: I think EoTV's view of instrumentality is not just healthy, it's so good for humanity that words like healthy cease to have meaning. The singularity is a truely radical game-changer, and I think Misato (and anyone who views it negatively) are only against this development out of fear, complacency and ignorance.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Sun May 18, 2014 11:55 pm Eva is illustrative, not definitive. The two endings show two polar opposite outcomes, but they are fundamentally the same. EoTV ends with Shinji committing existential suicide via lotus eating. EoE picks up directly where EoTV ends off. Shinji's little fantasy world of everyone clapping for him is directly contrasted with jerking off. In EoE Shinji resists the abyss when it looks back through him. He turns back from the leap of faith and does not cross, and is sucked into the left hand path. One is a solipsistic nightmare, the other is suicide via rejection of evolution. Shinji is the Last Man. Or, as I said once before, EoTV is the 3 AM moment of clarity when you see exactly what needs to be done to fix all your problems and EoE is when you wake up the next day at noon, peel your boxers away from your semen crusted penis, eat peppermint patties and lance crackers for breakfast and go on doing the same shit you solved last night. On a broader level, Eva is a fin-de-siècle work exploring the popular mid 90's impulse to consider then then current state of world affairs to be the end of history in a dialectical universal history sense and personify various reactions to that zeitgeist in different incarnations of the Last Man. EoE exists in part to reject EoTV, to make it clear that the singularity aka the nerd rapture is a bullshit fantasy that will never happen and would in fact be existential death for the human species. An ever-still monomind with no needs, wants, or change may as well be a rock, it's pointless. The final conclusion is that transhumanism is bullshit, liberal democratic society is bullshit, and that adulthood has died and left the world full of spoiled children of spoiled children where no one grows up, all with a slight undercurrent of Japanese psuedofascist authoritarian conservatism flavoring the meal, like an exotic spice use sparingly. That's what kimochi warui means. "I'm not your mom, stop crying on my tits, grow up." What disgusts her so much is that after she expresses some concern for him by stroking his cheek, he immediately reverts to trying to use her as an object and make her his surrogate mother. Asuka knows the riddle of steel.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Mon May 19, 2014 12:56 am NEMZ: There might be a language gap there. Though Episode 1 establishes that it is Eva that Shinji shouldn't be running away from. Yes, the circumstances were drastically different at the time. And the show does change Eva as something that should be considered a crutch, or at least it becomes a red herring in Shinji's character progression. But Episode 1 establishes Eva as the central object to be running from enough to narratively focus on the fact that Shinji is abandoning Eva in Episode 26. As for Impact being healthy for humanity, if Rei and Kaworu did embody love and hope in Instrumentallity, then Shinji might have been wrong to walk away from that when he ran from himself in EoE. (Though, it's suggested that one defines himself by the presence of others. So running from others is the same as Shinji running from himself.) And I don't see how it's unhealthy be with love and hope.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Mon May 19, 2014 1:14 am Shinji is pretty much the Lone Mall Goth of the Apocalypse.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Mon May 19, 2014 2:09 am Split out a tangent of this tangent to another recent thread perhaps a bit better suited for it, [url]http://forum.evageeks.org/thread/16201/Kimochi-warui.[/url] Things relevent here that were misplaced in this secondary operation: Mr. Tines wrote: Chuckman wrote: Eva is illustrative, not definitive. The two endings show two polar opposite outcomes, but they are fundamentally the same. EoTV ends with Shinji committing existential suicide via lotus eating. EoE picks up directly where EoTV ends off. Shinji's little fantasy world of everyone clapping for him is directly contrasted with jerking off. In EoE Shinji resists the abyss when it looks back through him. He turns back from the leap of faith and does not cross, and is sucked into the left hand path. One is a solipsistic nightmare, the other is suicide via rejection of evolution. Shinji is the Last Man.

[]:             Quote Post by SEELE » Mon May 19, 2014 6:06 am Chuckman wrote: The final conclusion is that transhumanism is bullshit, liberal democratic society is bullshit, and that adulthood has died and left the world full of spoiled children of spoiled children where no one grows up, all with a slight undercurrent of Japanese psuedofascist authoritarian conservatism flavoring the meal, like an exotic spice use sparingly.
